input.counterSource: Safari 26 shipped CSS Grid Lanes in stable (early 2026), which could be used to argue the feature has at least one stable implementation. However, this does not refute the claim — "not Baseline" requires broad cross-browser support, and with Chrome and Firefox still behind flags, the claim remains accurate. The syntax shift from `masonry` to `grid-lanes` also means any older flag-based Firefox support is not interoperable with the new standard.
input.evidence: The claim is well-supported by current evidence as of July 2026. The MDN source quote is accurate: CSS masonry (now standardized as "CSS Grid Lanes" with `display: grid-lanes`) is NOT at Baseline. As of July 2026, only Safari 26 ships it in a stable release; Chrome and Firefox still have it behind experimental flags. The claim that it "does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers" is factually correct — Chrome and Firefox (together representing the large majority of global browser market share) do not ship it in stable builds. The underlying spec even changed syntax (from `grid-template-rows: masonry` to `display: grid-lanes`), further confirming the technology is still in flux. Multiple credible primary sources corroborate this: caniuse.com, Chrome for Developers blog, WebKit blog, CSS-Tricks, and Smashing Magazine all confirm partial/experimental support only. The recommendation to use `@supports` progressive enhancement rather than relying on native masonry in production is consistently repeated across all credible sources. The source (MDN) is authoritative for this type of browser-compatibility claim. The claim is current, not outdated.
